OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":19,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"full-time student","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"upper secondary","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"curious","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["gardening","hiking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":82,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":31,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":83,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":19,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":5,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"Growing up in Washington DC, I’ve witnessed how quickly misinformation can spread across the city’s bustling streets and, more importantly, across the endless scroll of my phone. As a 19‑year‑old full‑time student who spends several hours a day on social media, I’ve learned that the first line of defense is curiosity—questioning every headline before it becomes part of my narrative. My high openness fuels this habit: I enjoy exploring new ideas, but I also crave the truth behind them. Yet my low conscientiousness can sometimes make me lazy about double‑checking facts, so I’ve had to develop quick mental habits: pause, search the source, and cross‑reference with reputable outlets like The New York Times or the Associated Press. When a post captures my attention, I tend to ask, “Who benefits if this is true?” This skeptical lens, a product of my low agreeableness, helps me spot sensationalism. I also lean on my Christian values of honesty and stewardship—misinformation feels like a violation of both. I’ve started to curate my feed by following fact‑checking organizations such as Snopes, PolitiFact, and the Poynter Institute, and I use browser extensions that flag known fake news sites. When the content feels doubtful, I discuss it with friends or classmates; our extraverted nature means we exchange ideas freely, and that collective scrutiny often reveals hidden biases. If I encounter a blatant hoax, I use the “report” function—it’s a small but essential act that contributes to the platform’s moderation ecosystem. Ultimately, managing fake content is a continuous practice of self‑education, community engagement, and intentional digital hygiene. By staying curious, questioning, and leveraging trustworthy sources, I can transform my social media experience from a passive echo chamber into an informed, critical conversation.\n","reasoning_summary":"I analyzed the persona traits: a 19‑year‑old male, curious, high openness, low conscientiousness, high extraversion, low agreeableness, low neuroticism, Christian, spends many hours on social media, hobbies gardening and hiking. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Growing up in Washington DC, I’ve witnessed how quickly misinformation can spread across the city’s bustling streets and, more importantly, across the endless scroll of my phone. As a 19‑year‑old full‑time student who spends several hours a day on social media, I’ve learned that the first line of defense is curiosity—questioning every headline before it becomes part of my narrative. My high openness fuels this habit: I enjoy exploring new ideas, but I also crave the truth behind them. Yet my low conscientiousness can sometimes make me lazy about double‑checking facts, so I’ve had to develop quick mental habits: pause, search the source, and cross‑reference with reputable outlets like The New York Times or the Associated Press. When a post captures my attention, I tend to ask, “Who benefits if this is true?” This skeptical lens, a product of my low agreeableness, helps me spot sensationalism. I also lean on my Christian values of honesty and stewardship—misinformation feels like a violation of both. I’ve started to curate my feed by following fact‑checking organizations such as Snopes, PolitiFact, and the Poynter Institute, and I use browser extensions that flag known fake news sites. When the content feels doubtful, I discuss it with friends or classmates; our extraverted nature means we exchange ideas freely, and that collective scrutiny often reveals hidden biases. If I encounter a blatant hoax, I use the “report” function—it’s a small but essential act that contributes to the platform’s moderation ecosystem. Ultimately, managing fake content is a continuous practice of self‑education, community engagement, and intentional digital hygiene. By staying curious, questioning, and leveraging trustworthy sources, I can transform my social media experience from a passive echo chamber into an informed, critical conversation.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I analyzed the persona traits: a 19‑year‑old male, curious, high openness, low conscientiousness, high extraversion, low agreeableness, low neuroticism, Christian, spends many hours on social media, hobbies gardening and hiking. These traits suggest an energetic, questioning tone with a focus on personal experience. I crafted a 300‑word continuous prose opinion that incorporates curiosity, skepticism, and faith. I cited reputable sources and included safety notes as none. I listed all persona fields referenced in the opinion. The JSON structure adheres to the required format, with no bullet points, single line, and proper field names.
